text: states! Just try not to get too attached to them or think they’re something they’re not. Love, learn, and let go. But for those of us who have eaten the apple, tasted the forbidden fruit, and been transformed by it—is there anything beyond? Are we just to go on loving and letting go? Or is there something beyond the holiest of spiritual states? Sure there is. First, states get increasingly subtle, so much so that they approach omnipresence. The jhanas are instructive in this regard. The first jhana is pretty over-the-top, filled with intense rapture. Then that gets too coarse, and the second jhana takes over, with pleasure and delight and amazing, shimmering light, but without some of the intense concentrated effort. Eventually even that gets coarse, and the mind moves to the third jhana, with pleasure and bliss, but not rapture and amazement. And eventually, even the love and bliss of the third jhana gets a bit coarse, and the mind moves into the fourth jhana, which is equanimous, transparent, and so subtle it’s barely there. By refining spiritual states, you don’t need ecstasy to feel connected anymore. Your mind (maybe your brain) learns to feel the love without the bells and whistles. Eventually, the whole nature of the search shifts, away from getting the biggest high and toward a kind of spiritual slipperiness, wherein you can come back to a restful, nondesiring mind faster and faster. In my case, a relaxed mind that used to take four days, concentration, and ecstasy to bring about can now be accessed within about three seconds. I can tell you, that is extremely, profoundly useful. Call it what you will—the light, nirvana, God, the mind, consciousness, emptiness, whatever—it’s almost always accessible. “The point is to see the light in everyone and everything,” the American-Tibetan teacher Lama Surya Das told me. That’s just right—and the “light” isn’t necessarily radiant, shining, first-jhana light, but just the ordinary light that is there all the time. Nothing special—and yet, with enough practice, just as special as that which is most special. Sorry if that seems cryptic. I’m not trying to be mysterious; this is just my experience. So practice evolves. We start with the mundane, then experience the sublime, then come back to the mundane, yet different. This happens over and over in our ordinary lives; long spiritual retreats are just that phenomenon magnified and transposed. It’s how the heart grows wiser (Jack Kornfield’s words): running and returning between ordinary and extraordinary. To be clear, returning to the ordinary has its own pitfalls. Theoretically, one can be a fully awakened, enlightened human being and still be a total schmuck. Enlightenment does not have to do with being a nice person; it’s about seeing through the veil of illusion, knowing all things to be totally conditioned and transitory and thus unclingable. Being a good person is something different, and enlightenment doesn’t really help with it. So this is not a magic wand. It also takes a lot of commitment, and I recognize my own privilege and good fortune in having been able to take five months out of my life to go on long retreats, and to have a flexible schedule even now. This is why monks are monks and not householders; other important commitments like family, career, and social-justice activism make it hard to find the time to do the work needed to really change the mind. The jhanas taught me that all I’d ever wanted—experiences of mystical union, bliss, and beyond—weren’t enough. Or rather, that they weren’t “it.” The point lies elsewhere, and yet, ironically, right here. So while I often try to conclude my essays with some poetic image or poignant anecdote, actually, whatever it is you’re looking at now is the true scenery for your enlightenment. So I’d rather not write any conclusion at all. Get it? RESOURCES ON JHANA PRACTICE The website of one of my teachers, Leigh Brasington: www.leighb.com/ The website of American students of Pa Auk Sayadaw who now teach on their own: www.jhanasadvice.com/ The Pali Canon on jhanas: www.palikanon.com/english/the_jhanas/jhanas00.htm Brahm, Ajahn. Mindfulness, Bliss, and Beyond . Boston: Wisdom Publications, 2006. Catherine, Shaila. Focused and Fearless . Boston: Wisdom Publications, 2008. Snyder, Stephen, and Tina Rasmussen. Practicing the Jhanas: Traditional Concentration Meditation as Presented by the Venerable Pa Auk Sayadaw . Boston: Shambhala, 2009. 7 DIAMOND SOLITAIRE: WASHING BEETS WITH GOD ERIK DAVIS A little over a decade ago, I had a bona fide, grade A, no-shit “mystical” experience—or at least something that felt a hell of a lot like a mystical experience. I have never written about it before and don’t talk about it much, but I’ve been thinking about it lately and thought I’d give it a shot here—ineffability and scare quotes and all. The deal went down, absurdly enough, during a month-long retreat at a Zen center in northern California; even more absurdly, it happened while I was washing a bunch of beets in the garden. I had come to the center to recover and reorient after the agony of finishing the final draft of Techgnosis . I was pretty wrung out. During work period one afternoon, I found myself alone in a shady corner field, rinsing a pile of freshly unearthed beets in a freestanding outside basin. I stood there, in the cool but sunny air, washing big clumps of moist, fragrant earth from the red roots. I hadn’t had any alcohol or drugs in weeks. What happened next is tough to describe, and I think I need to lay down a bit of background first. One idea you’ll find in esoteric psychology (and elsewhere) is the notion that there is a vital difference between the content of consciousness—sensations, feelings, perceptions, thoughts, and so forth—and the subject that perceives or, better, witnesses these feelings and perceptions. On the surface, the Witness might seem to be needlessly “dualistic”—a redeployment of the Cartesian split between mind and body that everybody is always bitching about. But it’s still kind
